title: Modeling context-dependent RNA splicing by deep learning
data_sources: RNA-binding protein expression profiles (likely GTEx/ENCODE; in companion paper)

data_scope.species: human (implied by references to disease and RBP studies)
data_scope.genome_build: not stated in this commentary; see companion paper
data_scope.rbp_panel: not specified in this commentary; see companion paper
data_scope.data_availability_note: All primary dataset details (accessions, sample counts, tissue coverage) reside in companion research article

core_conclusion: HELIX predicts full-length transcript isoform usage across diverse biological contexts by integrating genomic sequence features with RNA-binding protein expression. A splice-site-level model infers context-dependent splice-site signals; a bidirectional LSTM then aggregates these into relative isoform abundance — enabling context-sensitive predictions beyond sequence-only approaches.
why_it_matters: Moves splicing prediction beyond pure sequence (SpliceAI/Pangolin) by incorporating RBP expression as a context variable; operates at the transcript level to predict isoform ratios rather than isolated splice-site scores; provides a modular framework for assessing disease variant effects across cell types.
why_nature: Principled ML architecture novelty (two-level hierarchical design integrating heterogeneous biological inputs); broad relevance of alternative splicing to disease genetics and pharmacogenomics; companion News & Views amplifies the co-published HELIX research article in the same issue.
added_value_to_my_research: Foundation-model embedding as context vector parallels geospatial foundation model fine-tuning for spatial prediction tasks
Gradient-based attribution on RBP context mirrors SHAP-based explainability in my spatial ML models
Grammar-constrained LSTM loss is the sequence-biology analogue of physics-informed graph learning with conservation priors
Multi-omic context fusion blueprint maps to my multi-source data fusion (remote sensing + mobile + census + POI) challenge
Discriminative-to-generative inversion (design sequences for target isoform ratios) parallels converting accessibility predictors into facility placement designers
